chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper references its source code and specification in a GitLab repository ([39]), which serves as the working link for the implementation of MIRADOR.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation uses a prototype implemented in Java, comparing it against BFT-SMaRt. The performance section describes running experiments on five physical servers (8 cores, 3.60 GHz, 16 GB RAM), indicating a real hardware deployment/testbed environment.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper explicitly defines the fault model in Section V: 'MIRADOR tolerates up to f Byzantine faults in each cluster and relies on cluster sizes of at most 3 f + 1 micro replicas.' This is a clear, formal definition.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides rigorous, data-driven analysis by comparing latency (Figure 10) and throughput/latency under YCSB workloads (Figure 11), quantifying the performance trade-offs between MIRADOR's pull-based nature versus BFT-SMaRt's push-based approach.

major_weakness: The paper is exceptionally strong across all metrics; the primary weakness is minor and relates to the scope of its evaluation compared to the complexity of BFT systems.
